name: Churel
alias: Chudail · the backward-footed ghost of a woman dead in childbirth, who lures kinsmen and drains their youth
body: You see the beauty before you see anything wrong, a young woman waiting at the village edge or at the cremation ground at dusk, lovely in a way that makes a man forget the hour and the smoke still rising from the ghats. She smiles, and the smile is warm, and her bangles catch the last light as she lifts a hand, and only if you look down do you find the warning: her feet are turned backward, heels where the toes should be, so that her footprints walk away from the very place she stands. Some say the giveaway is also in the voice, too sweet, or in hair left loose and unkempt where a living woman would have bound it. By the time you have understood any of this she has already taken your arm, and her fingers are colder than the evening can account for.

She is born of grief and grievance, the spirit of a woman who died in childbirth or in pregnancy, or who was neglected, mistreated, or denied her proper funeral rites by her own household. Such a death is held to be impure, and the impurity festers into hunger. The churel returns disguised as someone desirable, often to entrap the very men of the family that failed her, the brothers and husbands and sons, leading them out into the dark near water or a burning ground and feeding there on their vitality. A man she takes is found at dawn wasted and white-haired, aged forty years in a single night, emptied of exactly the youth she herself was owed and never given. The bargain at her heart is brutal and fair in its own grammar: she takes back from the living what the living took from her. Against her the protections are all rites of belated respect. Women lost to such deaths were given careful, sometimes unusual burials so the soul would settle: the body laid face down, the feet bound or nailed, iron set in the grave, or mustard seeds scattered along the path home so that the returning spirit must stop and count each one and never reach the door. To name her, and to perform at last the funeral she was denied, is the deeper cure.

What lingers is the arithmetic of neglect, the sense that she is less a monster than a debt come due, a reckoning aimed back at the people who wronged her. The tale is told from India and Pakistan to Bangladesh and Nepal, with cousins as far afield as the pontianak of the islands and the churile of the Indian-Caribbean diaspora, a wailing figure in white who haunts the silk-cotton trees; her one constant signature, in every telling, is the backward feet. Old women still warn that you must not accept the kindness of a stranger met at twilight near water, and that you should always, always look down at the feet first. A man who survives her remembers her hands as cold and her voice as gentle, and remembers most of all the morning light in the mirror showing him a face decades older than the one he carried to bed.
note: The protections are rites of respect for the dead. Across the region, women who died in childbirth or other "unclean" deaths were given careful, sometimes unusual burials so the soul would rest: bodies might be buried face down, the feet bound or nailed, iron placed in the grave, or mustard seeds scattered along the path home so the returning spirit would be compelled to count them and never reach the door. Naming her and performing the proper funeral rites she was denied is the deeper cure.

The churel (also chudail, churail, or churreyl) is told across India, Pakistan, Bangladesh, and Nepal, with cousins in wider South and Southeast Asian lore such as the pontianak. The backward feet are her constant signature. The tale carries an older social weight: it grew up around the real fear and stigma surrounding maternal death and the mistreatment of low-status or widowed women, so that the ghost is, in part, a reckoning aimed back at the household that wronged her.

recountings.text: A common retelling has a man riding alone through the mountains at night who stops for a woman stranded at the roadside. She climbs on and sits silently behind him, then vanishes the moment they pass another traveler. When he turns to look, all he glimpses are her backward-pointing feet slipping into the dark. This is widely-circulated lore rather than one person's account, presented here as the classic Pichal Peri (a regional form of the churel) story pattern.

A writer recalls that his uncle used to share unverifiable stories of meeting the Pichal Peri while driving late at night on lonely Pakistani highways. In the uncle's telling, a beautiful woman would flag him down for help and then vanish the instant he was distracted; her one giveaway was that her feet were always turned backward. Locals explained her as the vengeful ghost of a woman who died in childbirth or was abused by in-laws. This is a specific person's family recollection, not anonymous lore.
recountings.attribution: INKspire, Urban Legends From My Childhood in Pakistan

recountings.text: In the Indian-Caribbean diaspora the spirit survives as the churile, retold by Trinidadian folklorist Marsha Gomes-McKie as the ghost of a woman who died pregnant or in childbirth. She wanders lonely roads and silk cotton trees as a wailing figure in white with unkempt hair and, in some versions, backward feet or a head turned upside down, carrying an infant whose cry sounds like a cat. Unlike the South Asian original she preys mainly on pregnant women and babies, and crossing a river is said to escape her. This is a regional retelling of the lore, not a firsthand encounter.
recountings.attribution: Marsha Gomes-McKie, Caribbean Folklore Part 7: Churile

references.title: Pichal Peri: The Reverse Footed Ghost
Urban Legends From My Childhood in Pakistan
